dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems to a 57-year-old female with COPD.
Hi, I'm Alex, a paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what brought you to call 911 today?
I've been short of breath for about a week, but it got really bad in the last two hours.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where you are?
My name is Elaine. I'm at home in [CITY].
You appear alert and oriented x4. Can you state the year and city?
2019 and I'm in [CITY].
Can you squeeze my fingers upon command? Please squeeze my left finger.
(Squeezes finger)
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-AOx4.
Your lung sounds were clear in all fields with no wheezing or crackles.
Your breathing was very rapid at 37 per minute initially and now is down to 27.
Your airway is clear and patent. You show no signs of obstruction.
Your pulse started at 111 and dropped to 90, which is still on the higher side but stable.
Your oxygen level was very low at 78% but has improved with high-flow oxygen. It's now 98%.
Your blood pressure was 168 over 68 and is now 146 over 78.
Your end-tidal CO2 was 23 when we first arrived, which suggests blow-by CO2 and respiratory distress.
Your blood glucose was reported as 0-likely a technical error, we'll monitor as we go along.
Your temperature was 98.6°F-no fever noted.
Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-eye opening to speech, appropriate verbal response, and obeying motor commands.
You were short of breath for a week before it worsened two hours ago.
You take Symbicort regularly for your COPD, but reported no albuterol use in the time of worsening symptoms.
You had not used your albuterol when the breathing problem became severe.
When was the last time you ate or drank something?
About four hours ago.
Your past medical history includes a diagnosis of COPD.
Your skin was not pale or diaphoretic.
You appeared adequately perfused.
We placed an IV in your left hand for possible fluid and medication support.
A 12-lead EKG was completed. The interpretation was sinus rhythm with no STE or ectopy noted.
You're getting 12 liters of oxygen via a non-rebreather mask for support.
Your cardiac rhythm is sinus with no acute changes.
Your second set of vital signs shows significant improvement-oxygen is now with 98% saturation.
Respiratory effort is down to 27 per minute with better breathing comfort.
Heart rate has dropped to 90 and blood pressure at 146 over 78-no major changes in circulation.
You're heading to Community Hospital unless you have a preferred facility.
I want to go to Community Hospital please.
We will have you transferred to Community Hospital with a standing and pivot method then secured on the stretcher.
All signs point to stable findings for transfer-respiratory improvement and no pain reported.
We've noted that you are Elaine, 57 years old, and were found at your home in [CITY].
You are known to have an allergy to codeine and take Symbicort daily.
No known allergies to medications currently in use and no specimen collected en route.
Ready for ED transfer-spO2 at 98%, stable pulse, and vitals.
Richards Community Hospital is inbound. We will transport you there with care.
We did not note any pain on our assessment.
No abnormal skin turgor or rashes were noted.
Your neck showed no signs of rigidity or lymphadenopathy.
No drainage, obstruction, or foreign body was observed in your airway.
Breath sounds remained clear, and no abnormal heart sounds were heard.
Your abdomen appeared normal with no tenderness, rigidity, or guarding.
No deformity or angulation in your extremities was found.
No abnormalities in urination frequency or rectal bleeding were reported.
We are following the COPD exacerbation protocol (Protocol 3-11) per standing orders.
Your pulse is 90, respiration is 27, blood pressure 146/78, and spO2 is 98%.
No further medications or procedures are needed at this time.
No new pain symptoms, and your comfort seems stable.
You remain non-specific in your COPD symptoms, with no new events reported.
You have no known new allergies and no recent oral intake beyond four hours ago.
You're secured and ready for ED room admission.
We have all your information ready for the emergency team in room 5.
